sentences.chinese: 教化廢而奸邪並出，刑罰不能勝者，其堤防壞也。
sentences.literal: When transformation through instruction is cast aside and treachery and deviance all emerge together, and punishments cannot overcome it, its dykes and dams are ruined.
sentences.idiomatic: When moral education fails and vice runs riot, no amount of punishment can hold the flood—the levees have burst.

sentences.chinese: 古之王者明於此，是故南面而治天下，莫不以教化為大務。
sentences.literal: The kings of antiquity understood this; therefore in facing south they governed all under Heaven, and none did not take transformation through instruction as the great task.
sentences.idiomatic: The sage kings knew this, so every ruler who sat facing south made teaching his first duty.

sentences.chinese: 立太學以教於國，設癢序以化於邑，漸民以仁，摩民以誼，節民以禮，故其刑罰甚輕而禁不犯者，教化行而習俗美也。
sentences.literal: They established the Imperial Academy to instruct the state, set up schools to transform the districts, gradually imbued the people with benevolence, rubbed them with righteousness, restrained them with ritual—therefore although their punishments were very light and prohibitions were not violated, it was because transformation through instruction was practiced and customs were beautiful.
sentences.idiomatic: They founded the Imperial Academy for the capital, local schools for the towns, and there steeped the people in kindness, polished them with duty, and trimmed their conduct with rites—so penalties could stay light and the law stay unbroken, because custom had been shaped and manners made fair.

sentences.chinese: 聖王之繼亂世也，掃除其跡而悉去之，復修教化而崇起之。
sentences.literal: When sage-kings succeed to a chaotic age, they sweep away its traces and entirely remove them, again cultivate transformation through instruction and exalt and raise it.
sentences.idiomatic: When a true king inherits chaos, he clears away its traces, then rebuilds moral education and lifts it high again.

sentences.chinese: 教化已明，習俗已成，子孫循之，行五六百歲尚未敗也。
sentences.literal: When transformation through instruction is already clear and customs are already formed, descendants follow them, and for five or six hundred years they have still not declined.
sentences.idiomatic: Once teaching is plain and habit fixed, heirs can follow the pattern five or six hundred years without ruin.

sentences.chinese: 至周之末世，大為亡道，以失天下。
sentences.literal: Reaching the last generation of Zhou, they greatly practiced loss of the Way, and thereby lost all under Heaven.
sentences.idiomatic: By Zhou’s last days they had abandoned the Way outright and forfeited the realm.

sentences.chinese: 秦繼其後，獨不能改，又益甚之，重禁文學，不得挾書，棄捐禮誼而惡聞之，其心欲盡滅先聖之道，而顓為自恣苟簡之治，故立為天子十四歲而國破亡矣。
sentences.literal: Qin succeeded them, alone was unable to change, and again made it worse, heavily forbidding letters and learning, not allowing carrying of books, cast aside ritual and righteousness and hated hearing of them; their intent desired entirely to extinguish the way of the former sages, and solely to conduct government of self-indulgent rough simplicity—therefore having been established as Son of Heaven fourteen years, the state was broken and lost.
sentences.idiomatic: Qin followed and only dug the hole deeper: it banned study and books, spurned ritual and right, and meant to wipe out the sages’ teaching in favor of capricious, makeshift rule—so within fourteen years of taking the throne the dynasty was gone.

sentences.chinese: 自古以來，未嘗有以亂濟亂，大敗天下之民如秦者也。
sentences.literal: From antiquity to the present, there has never been one who used chaos to rescue chaos and greatly defeated the people of all under Heaven like Qin.
sentences.idiomatic: Never has a dynasty used disorder to cure disorder or broken its people as thoroughly as Qin.

sentences.chinese: 其遺毒余烈，至今未滅，使習俗薄惡，人民□頑，抵冒殊扞，孰爛如此之甚者也。
sentences.literal: Its remaining poison and lingering fierceness have to the present not been extinguished, causing customs to be thin and evil, the people [one character missing in text] stubborn, to collide and offend and resist extraordinarily—who has rotted to such an extreme?
sentences.idiomatic: Its toxin still lingers, souring custom and hardening hearts until men brawl against every restraint—nothing has corroded a people quite so far.

sentences.chinese: 孔子曰：「腐朽之木不可雕也，糞土之牆不可圬也。」
sentences.literal: Confucius said: “Rotten wood cannot be carved; a wall of dung-soil cannot be plastered.”
sentences.idiomatic: Confucius said, “Rotten wood will not take the knife; a dung-plastered wall will not take the trowel.”

sentences.chinese: 今漢繼秦之後，如朽木、糞牆矣，雖欲善治之，亡可奈何。
sentences.literal: Now Han succeeds Qin; it is like rotten wood and a dung wall—although one desires to govern it well, there is nothing that can be done.
sentences.idiomatic: Han has inherited Qin’s mess—rotted timber and a crumbling wall—so even the wish to rule well meets what cannot be mended by small repairs.

sentences.chinese: 法出而奸生，令下而詐起，如以湯止沸，抱薪救火，愈甚亡益也。
sentences.literal: When laws issue, treachery is born; when orders descend, deceit arises—it is like using hot broth to stop boiling, or embracing firewood to save from fire: the more so, the more without benefit.
sentences.idiomatic: Laws breed evasion the moment they are posted, and orders breed fraud the moment they drop—like pouring boiling water on a boil or fighting a blaze with kindling: the harder you try, the worse it gets.

sentences.chinese: 竊譬之琴瑟不調，甚者必解而更張之，乃可鼓也；
sentences.literal: Your servant privately compares it: when zither and se are not tuned, in extreme cases one must loosen and re-string them, then they can be played;
sentences.idiomatic: Think of a lute far out of tune: sometimes you must slack the strings and reseat them before it will sound true;

sentences.chinese: 為政而不行，甚者必變而更化之，乃可理也。
sentences.literal: when conducting government and it does not go, in extreme cases one must change and transform it, then it can be ordered.
sentences.idiomatic: likewise a policy that will not run must be remade root and branch before the state can be set right.

sentences.chinese: 當更張而不更張，雖有良工不能善調也：當更化而不更化，雖有大賢不能善治也。
sentences.literal: When it is time to re-string yet one does not re-string, although there is a good craftsman he cannot tune well; when it is time to transform yet one does not transform, although there is a great worthy he cannot govern well.
sentences.idiomatic: If the strings need resetting and you refuse, no master can tune them; if the times demand reform and you refuse, no sage can govern.

sentences.chinese: 故漢得天下以來，常欲善治而至今不可善治者，失之於當更化而不更化也。
sentences.literal: Therefore since Han obtained all under Heaven, it has constantly desired to govern well, yet to the present cannot govern well—because it failed in the matter of transforming when transformation was due.
sentences.idiomatic: Han has wanted good order since the founding, yet never reached it—because it would not change when change was due.

sentences.chinese: 古人有言曰：「臨淵羨魚，不如退而結網。」
sentences.literal: The ancients had a saying: “Standing by the pool envying the fish is not as good as withdrawing and knotting the net.”
sentences.idiomatic: The old proverb says, “Better to mend your net by the stream than only to sigh at the fish.”

sentences.chinese: 今臨政而願治七十餘歲矣，不如退而更化；
sentences.literal: Now facing government and desiring order for more than seventy years—it is not as good as withdrawing and transforming;
sentences.idiomatic: You have faced the realm and longed for order for seventy years; it is time to step back and refashion policy;

sentences.chinese: 更化則可善治，善治則災害日去，福祿日來。
sentences.literal: if one transforms, then one can govern well; if one governs well, then disasters daily depart and blessings and emoluments daily come.
sentences.idiomatic: reform brings good rule, good rule drives calamity off day by day and draws blessing in its place.

sentences.chinese: 《詩》云：「宜民宜人，受祿於人。」
sentences.literal: The Odes say: “Fit for the people, fit for the people—receive emolument from others.”
sentences.idiomatic: The Classic of Poetry says, “What suits the people wins Heaven’s pay.”

sentences.chinese: 為政而宜於民者，固當受祿於天。
sentences.literal: He who conducts government and is fitting for the people ought indeed to receive emolument from Heaven.
sentences.idiomatic: A ruler whose government truly serves the people may expect Heaven’s reward.

sentences.chinese: 夫仁、誼、禮、知、信五常之道，王者所當修飭也；
sentences.literal: As for the Way of the five constants—benevolence, righteousness, ritual, wisdom, and good faith—these are what the king ought to cultivate and set in order;
sentences.idiomatic: The five constants—humaneness, duty, ritual, wisdom, and good faith—are what a king must cultivate;

sentences.chinese: 五者修飭，故受天之晁，而享鬼神之靈，德施於方外，延及群生也。
sentences.literal: when the five are cultivated and set in order, therefore he receives Heaven’s radiance and enjoys the spirits’ numinous power, virtue extends beyond the borders and reaches the throng of the living.
sentences.idiomatic: when those five are kept in repair, Heaven’s light rests on him, the spirits attend, and his grace reaches beyond the frontiers to every living thing.

sentences.chinese: 天子覽其對而異焉，乃復冊之曰：
sentences.literal: The Son of Heaven reviewed his reply and marveled at it; then again enfeoffed him with a document, saying:
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor read Dong Zhongshu’s answer with wonder and sent a second rescript:

sentences.chinese: 制曰：蓋聞虞舜之時，游於巖郎之上，垂拱無為，而天下太平。
sentences.literal: The edict said: I have heard that in the time of Shun of Yu, he roamed above the rock gallery, letting his sash hang down and doing nothing, yet all under Heaven was peaceful.
sentences.idiomatic: The rescript ran: “They say Shun of Yu strolled the cliff galleries with folded hands and all beneath Heaven stayed at peace.

sentences.chinese: 周文王至於日昃不暇食，而宇內亦治。
sentences.literal: King Wen of Zhou reached the point where the sun was setting and had no leisure to eat, yet within the four seas there was also order.
sentences.idiomatic: King Wen of Zhou toiled past noon without pausing for a meal, yet the realm was still well ordered.

sentences.chinese: 夫帝王之道，豈不同條共貫與？
sentences.literal: As for the way of emperors and kings, is it not the same strip threading through?
sentences.idiomatic: Can the royal way be anything but one continuous thread?

sentences.chinese: 何逸勞之殊也？
sentences.literal: Why is the difference of ease and toil so extreme?
sentences.idiomatic: Why then do we see such opposite pictures of leisure and strain?

sentences.chinese: 蓋儉者不造玄黃旌旗之飾。
sentences.literal: It is said the frugal do not make black-and-yellow, pennant-and-banner adornments.
sentences.idiomatic: The frugal king does not pile on dark silks and gaudy banners.

sentences.chinese: 及至周室，設兩觀，乘大路，硃干玉戚，八佾陳於庭，而頌聲興。
sentences.literal: Reaching the house of Zhou, they set up two outlooks, rode the great road, vermilion shield and jade battle-axe, eight rows of dancers arrayed in the court, and praise-songs arose.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhou went on to twin watchtowers, the great chariot, crimson shields and jade axes, eight files of dancers in the courtyard—and then the hymns rose.

sentences.chinese: 夫帝王之道豈異指哉？
sentences.literal: As for the way of emperors and kings, can the intent differ?
sentences.idiomatic: So can the royal way point two different ways?

sentences.chinese: 或曰良玉不瑑，又曰非文亡以輔德，二端異焉。
sentences.literal: Some say fine jade is not carved, and again say without pattern there is no means to assist virtue—the two ends diverge.
sentences.idiomatic: One maxim says leave fine jade uncarved; another says without ornament virtue cannot shine—those two teachings pull apart.

sentences.chinese: 殷人執五刑以督奸，傷肌膚以懲惡。
sentences.literal: The Yin people wielded the five punishments to supervise treachery, injuring the skin to punish evil.
sentences.idiomatic: The Shang used the five mutilating punishments to hunt down crime and flayed men to frighten evil.

sentences.chinese: 成、康不式，四十餘年天下不犯，囹圄空虛。
sentences.literal: Cheng and Kang did not take them as model; for more than forty years all under Heaven did not offend, and the prisons were empty.
sentences.idiomatic: Kings Cheng and Kang refused that model, and for forty years no one broke the law and the jails stood empty.

sentences.chinese: 秦國用之，死者甚眾，刑者相望，□矣哀哉！
sentences.literal: The state of Qin used them; the dead were very many, the punished looked at each other [along the road], □—alas!
sentences.idiomatic: Qin revived them: corpses piled up and convicts shuffled past one another in an endless line—how bitter a sight!

sentences.chinese: 烏乎！
sentences.literal: Wuhu!
sentences.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.chinese: 朕夙寤晨興，惟前帝王之憲，永思所以奉至尊，章洪業，皆在力本任賢。
sentences.literal: I rise early from sleep and rise at dawn, ponder the statutes of the former emperors and kings, and ever think how thereby to serve the supreme and manifest the great enterprise—all lies in forcing the root and employing the worthy.
sentences.idiomatic: I wake before dawn and brood on the laws of the ancient kings, seeking how to serve the throne and magnify the great work—knowing it all hangs on strengthening the roots and trusting the worthy.

sentences.chinese: 今朕親耕籍田以為農先，勸孝弟，崇有德，使者冠蓋相望，問勤勞，恤孤獨，盡思極神，功烈休德未始雲獲也。
sentences.literal: Now I personally plough the sacred field to be first for farming, encourage filial piety and fraternal duty, honor the virtuous; envoys’ canopies and covers look at each other on the road, inquire into diligence and toil, pity the orphaned and alone—I exhaust thought and spirit to the utmost, yet meritorious deeds and blessed virtue have not yet begun to be obtained.
sentences.idiomatic: I have plowed the consecrated field to lead the farmers, honored filial devotion and the virtuous, sent a stream of envoys to ask after the weary and succor the lone—yet for all this outpouring of care, no matching blessing has appeared.

sentences.chinese: 今陰陽錯繆，氛氣充塞，群生寡遂，黎民未濟，廉恥貿亂，賢不肖渾淆，未得其真，故詳延特起之士，庶幾乎！
sentences.literal: Now yin and yang are twisted awry, vapors fill and block, the throng of the living seldom attain their ends, the black-haired people are not saved, integrity and shame are traded in confusion, worthy and unworthy are muddled together—I have not obtained the truth; therefore I have detailedly summoned specially raised men—perhaps!
sentences.idiomatic: Yin and yang are out of joint, foul air chokes the sky, creatures barely thrive and the common people flounder, honor and shame are traded like goods, worthies blur with fools—I still lack the truth, so I cast the net wide for exceptional men in hope of an answer.

sentences.chinese: 今子大夫待詔百有餘人，或道世務而未濟，稽諸上古之不同，考之於今而難行，毋乃牽於文系而不得騁與？
sentences.literal: Now you, great officers, await edict, more than a hundred persons—some discourse on worldly affairs yet do not succeed, check against high antiquity and it is not the same, examine in the present and it is hard to practice—is it perhaps that you are tied by literary bonds and cannot gallop?
sentences.idiomatic: You over a hundred await orders: some speak of the age yet miss the mark, some echo antiquity yet cannot apply it, some offer schemes that will not run in our day—are you tangled in empty formulae and unable to speak plain?

sentences.chinese: 將所繇異術，所聞殊方與？
sentences.literal: Or is it that the paths you follow are different and what you have heard is from different quarters?
sentences.idiomatic: Or do you follow different roads and listen to contrary schools?

sentences.chinese: 各悉對，著於篇，毋諱有司。
sentences.literal: Each fully answer, write it in the document, do not hide from the authorities.
sentences.idiomatic: Answer each point fully on the page and do not hold back for fear of the clerks.

sentences.chinese: 明其指略，切磋究之。
sentences.literal: Make clear its gist and outline, polish and investigate it.
sentences.idiomatic: Set out your meaning clearly, then refine and probe it.

sentences.chinese: 以稱朕意。
sentences.literal: So as to match my intent.
sentences.idiomatic: So that it may match what I intend.”

sentences.chinese: 仲舒對曰：
sentences.literal: Zhongshu replied, saying:
sentences.idiomatic: Dong Zhongshu answered:

sentences.chinese: 臣聞堯受命，以天下為憂，而未以位為樂也，故誅逐亂臣，務求賢聖，是以得舜、禹、稷、□、咎繇。
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard that when Yao received the mandate, he took all under Heaven as worry and did not take the position as joy; therefore he executed and drove away disorderly ministers, strove to seek the worthy and sage, and thereby obtained Shun, Yu, Ji, [one character missing in text], and Gaoyao.
sentences.idiomatic: I have heard that Yao, once charged with the realm, worried for the world rather than rejoiced in the throne—so he purged corrupt ministers and sought sages, gaining Shun, Yu, Lord Millet, Xie, and Gaoyao.

sentences.chinese: 眾聖輔德，賢能佐職，教化大行，天下和洽，萬民皆安仁樂誼，各得其宜，動作應禮，從容中道。
sentences.literal: The host of sages assisted virtue, the able assisted offices, transformation through instruction greatly flourished, all under Heaven was harmonious and concordant, the myriad people all were secure in benevolence and glad in righteousness, each obtained what was fitting, movements and actions accorded with ritual, leisurely they hit the mean.
sentences.idiomatic: Those sages aided his virtue and worthy men filled his offices, until teaching spread wide, the land was at peace, and every man moved with ease inside ritual’s bounds.

sentences.chinese: 故孔子曰：「如有王者，必世而後仁，」此之謂也。
sentences.literal: Therefore Confucius said: “If there were a true king, after a generation there would be benevolence”—this speaks of it.
sentences.idiomatic: Confucius said, “Give a true king one generation and kindness would fill the land”—he meant exactly this.

sentences.chinese: 堯在位七十載，乃遜於位以禪虞舜。
sentences.literal: Yao occupied the position seventy years, then yielded the position to abdicate to Shun of Yu.
sentences.idiomatic: Yao reigned seventy years, then ceded the throne to Shun.

sentences.chinese: 堯崩，天下不歸堯子丹硃而歸舜。
sentences.literal: When Yao died, all under Heaven did not return to Yao’s son Dan Zhu but returned to Shun.
sentences.idiomatic: When Yao died, the realm did not pass to his son Dan Zhu but rallied to Shun.

sentences.chinese: 舜知不可辟，乃即天子之位，以禹為相，因堯之輔佐，繼其統業，是以垂拱無為而天下治。
sentences.literal: Shun knew he could not avoid it, then took the position of Son of Heaven, made Yu minister, followed Yao’s assistants, continued his unified enterprise—therefore letting his sash hang down and doing nothing, yet all under Heaven was ordered.
sentences.idiomatic: Shun saw he could not refuse, mounted the throne, made Yu his minister, kept Yao’s counselors, and carried on the work—so he could fold his hands while the empire ran itself.

sentences.chinese: 孔子曰「《韶》盡美矣，又盡善矣」，此之謂也。
sentences.literal: Confucius said “The Shao is utterly beautiful, and also utterly good”—this speaks of it.
sentences.idiomatic: Confucius said of the Shao, “It is perfect in beauty and perfect in goodness”—he spoke of this age.

sentences.chinese: 至於殷紂，逆天暴物，殺戮賢知，殘賊百姓。
sentences.literal: As for Yin Zhou, he opposed Heaven, violently treated things, killed and slaughtered the worthy and wise, mutilated and injured the hundred surnames.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhou Xin of Shang defied Heaven, ravaged the world, butchered wise men, and tortured the people.

sentences.chinese: 伯夷、太公皆當世賢者，隱處而不為臣。
sentences.literal: Boyi and the Grand Duke were both worthies of the age, yet hid in retirement and did not become ministers.
sentences.idiomatic: Men like Boyi and the Grand Duke were the age’s finest, yet they hid and would not serve.

sentences.chinese: 守職之人皆奔走逃亡，入於河海。
sentences.literal: Those who held office all ran off in flight and entered the rivers and seas.
sentences.idiomatic: Officeholders fled wholesale to the rivers and coasts.

sentences.chinese: 天下□亂，萬民不安，故天下去殷而從周。
sentences.literal: All under Heaven was in chaos, the myriad people were not secure; therefore all under Heaven abandoned Yin and followed Zhou.
sentences.idiomatic: The realm fell into chaos and the people had no peace, so all under Heaven left Shang for Zhou.

sentences.chinese: 文王順天理物，師用賢聖，是以閎夭、大顛、散宜生等亦聚於朝廷。
sentences.literal: King Wen accorded with Heaven’s pattern and ordered things, took as teachers and used the worthy and sage; therefore Hongyao, Datian, Sanyisheng, and the like also gathered in the court.
sentences.idiomatic: King Wen followed Heaven’s pattern in ordering things and took sages as his teachers, so Hongyao, Datian, Sanyisheng, and their like filled his court.

sentences.chinese: 愛施兆民，天下歸之，故太公起海濱而即三公也。
sentences.literal: His love extended to the trillion people, all under Heaven returned to him; therefore the Grand Duke rose from the seashore and immediately became one of the three dukes.
sentences.idiomatic: His kindness reached every household, and the world turned to him—so the Grand Duke left his fishing rock and stepped straight into the three highest posts.

sentences.chinese: 當此之時，紂尚在上，尊卑昏亂，百姓散亡，故文王悼痛而欲安之，是以日昃而不暇食民。
sentences.literal: At this time Zhou Xin was still above, high and low were confused in disorder, the hundred surnames scattered and fled; therefore King Wen grieved in pain and desired to secure them—therefore when the sun was setting he had no leisure to eat [the character 民 appears in text].
sentences.idiomatic: Meanwhile Zhou Xin still sat on the throne, rank was upside down, and the people scattered in terror; King Wen grieved for them and strove to bring peace, laboring past sunset without time even to eat.

sentences.chinese: 孔子作《春秋》，先正王而系萬事，見素王之文焉。
sentences.literal: Confucius made the Chunqiu, first correcting the king and tying the myriad affairs, showing the pattern-text of the uncrowned king.
sentences.idiomatic: Confucius wrote the Spring and Autumn Annals, beginning with the true king and threading every event, revealing the charter of the “king without a throne.”

sentences.chinese: 由此觀之，帝王之條貫同，然而勞逸異者，所遇之時異也。
sentences.literal: From this it is viewed: the threading-through of emperors and kings is the same, yet ease and toil differ because the times they encountered differ.
sentences.idiomatic: From this we see one royal thread running through history; ease and toil differ only with the age.

sentences.chinese: 孔子曰「《武》盡美矣，未盡善也」，此之謂也。
sentences.literal: Confucius said “The Wu is utterly beautiful, not yet utterly good”—this speaks of it.
sentences.idiomatic: Confucius said of the Wu dance, “The form is perfect, the goodness not yet perfect”—he meant this harder age.

sentences.chinese: 臣聞制度文采玄黃之飾，所以明尊卑，異貴賤，而勸有德也。
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard that in institutions the pattern and color of black and yellow, the adornments of pennants and banners, are the means by which to clarify high and low, distinguish noble and base, and encourage those with virtue.
sentences.idiomatic: Rites, colors, banners, and silks exist to mark rank, separate noble from common, and reward virtue.

sentences.chinese: 故《春秋》受命所先制者，改正朔，易服色，所以應天也。
sentences.literal: Therefore what the Chunqiu, on receiving the mandate, first regulated was to correct the first month of the year, change garment color—so as to respond to Heaven.
sentences.idiomatic: The Annals, when Heaven appoints a king, begins by fixing the calendar and changing court dress—to answer Heaven’s rhythm.

sentences.chinese: 然則官至旌旗之制，有法而然者也。
sentences.literal: Then as for the regulation reaching to pennants and banners, there is law and thus it is so.
sentences.idiomatic: Hence regalia down to flags and streamers follows fixed law, not whim.

sentences.chinese: 故孔子曰：「奢則不遜，儉則固。」
sentences.literal: Therefore Confucius said: “Extravagance then is not deferential; frugality then is crude.”
sentences.idiomatic: Confucius said, “Luxury breeds rudeness; niggardliness leaves you mean.”

sentences.chinese: 儉非聖人之中制也。
sentences.literal: Frugality is not the mean regulation of the sage.
sentences.idiomatic: But miserliness is no middle path for a sage.

sentences.chinese: 臣聞良玉不□，資質潤美，不待刻□，此亡異於達巷黨人不學而自知也。
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard that fine jade is not carved—its substance is moist and beautiful, it does not await cutting and carving; this is no different from a man of Daxiang lane who without learning knows of himself.
sentences.idiomatic: They say fine jade need not be cut—yet that is like claiming a lad from Daxiang ward is wise without study.

sentences.chinese: 然則常玉不□，不成文章；
sentences.literal: Then as for ordinary jade, if it is not carved, it does not become pattern and text;
sentences.idiomatic: Ordinary stone, if uncut, shows no grain;

sentences.chinese: 君子不學，不成其德。
sentences.literal: if the gentleman does not learn, he does not complete his virtue.
sentences.idiomatic: the gentleman, if untutored, never finishes his virtue.

sentences.chinese: 臣聞聖王之治天下也，少則習之學，長則材諸位，爵祿以養其德，刑罰以威其惡，故民曉於禮誼而恥犯其上。
sentences.literal: Your servant has heard that when sage-kings ordered all under Heaven, in youth they accustomed them in learning, in maturity they placed them in offices, emoluments and ranks to nurture their virtue, punishments and penalties to awe their evil—therefore the people understood ritual and righteousness and were ashamed to offend those above them.
sentences.idiomatic: The sage kings taught the young in school, gave grown men posts, fed their character with salary, curbed crime with punishment—so the people knew ritual and blushed to rebel.

sentences.chinese: 武王行大誼，平殘賊，周公作禮樂以文之，至於成康之隆，囹圄空虛四十餘年，此亦教化之漸而仁誼之流，非獨傷肌膚之效也。
sentences.literal: King Wu practiced great righteousness, pacified the mutilating rebels, the Duke of Zhou made ritual and music to pattern them; reaching the height of Cheng and Kang, the prisons were empty for more than forty years—this also was the gradualness of transformation through instruction and the flowing of benevolence and righteousness, not alone the effect of injuring the skin.
sentences.idiomatic: King Wu cut down Zhou’s cruelty, and the Duke of Zhou set rites and music to refine the realm; by the peace of Cheng and Kang the jails stood empty forty years—because custom had shifted and kindness spread, not because men were merely flayed into fear.

sentences.chinese: 至秦則不然。
sentences.literal: Reaching Qin, it was not so.
sentences.idiomatic: Qin took the opposite road.

sentences.chinese: 師申商之法，行韓非之說，憎帝王之道，以貪狼為俗，非有文德以教訓於下也。
sentences.literal: They took as teacher the laws of Shen and Shang, practiced the doctrines of Han Fei, hated the way of emperors and kings, took greed and wolfishness as custom, and had no patterned virtue to instruct and train those below.
sentences.idiomatic: It copied Shang Yang’s statutes and Han Fei’s theory, spurned the kings’ Way, made greed a habit, and offered the people no moral pattern.

sentences.chinese: 誅名而不察實，為善者不必免，而犯惡者未必刑也。
sentences.literal: They investigated names and did not examine reality; those who did good did not necessarily escape, yet those who violated evil were not necessarily punished.
sentences.idiomatic: It chased labels instead of facts, so the good were not spared and the wicked were not sure to pay.

sentences.chinese: 是以百官皆飾虛辭而不顧實，外有事君之禮，內有背上之心；
sentences.literal: Therefore the hundred officials all adorned empty phrases and did not look to reality; outwardly they had the ritual of serving the lord, inwardly they had the mind of turning their backs on the lord;
sentences.idiomatic: Officials learned to mouth empty phrases while plotting disloyalty behind a show of duty;

sentences.chinese: 造偽飾詐，趣利無恥；
sentences.literal: they made falsehood and adorned deceit, hastened to profit without shame;
sentences.idiomatic: they forged, lied, and raced for gain without shame;

sentences.chinese: 又好用□酷之吏，賦斂亡度，竭民財力，百姓散亡，不得從耕織之業，群盜並起。
sentences.literal: again they liked to use cruel clerks, levies and exactions without measure, exhausted the people’s wealth and strength, the hundred surnames scattered and fled, could not follow the work of plowing and weaving, and bands of robbers arose together.
sentences.idiomatic: they loved cruel taxmen, taxed without limit, bled the people dry, drove farmers from their looms and fields, and let bandit gangs rise side by side.

sentences.chinese: 是以刑者甚眾，死者相望，而奸不息，俗化使然也。
sentences.literal: Therefore the punished were very many, the dead looked at each other, yet treachery did not cease—custom and transformation made it so.
sentences.idiomatic: Hence corpses and convicts choked the roads, yet crime never stopped—because habit had shaped them so.

sentences.chinese: 故孔子曰「導之以政，齊之以刑，民免而無恥」，此之謂也。
sentences.literal: Therefore Confucius said “Lead them with policies, align them with punishments, and the people will escape but have no shame”—this speaks of it.
sentences.idiomatic: Confucius said, “Drive the people with edicts, herd them with penalties, and they may stay out of jail but will feel no shame”—he meant Qin.

sentences.chinese: 今陛下並有天下，海內莫不率服，廣覽兼聽，極群下之知，盡天下之美，至德昭然，施於方外。
sentences.literal: Now Your Majesty possesses all together all under Heaven; within the seas none fail to submit and serve; broadly you look and together listen, exhaust the knowledge of the throng below, exhaust the beauty of all under Heaven; the utmost virtue is shining clear, extending beyond the borders.
sentences.idiomatic: You now hold the realm; none within the seas refuses allegiance; you listen wide and think deep, drawing on every talent and every good—your supreme virtue already shines beyond the borders.

sentences.chinese: 夜郎、康居，殊方萬里，說德歸誼，此太平之致也。
sentences.literal: Yelang and Kangju, distant regions ten thousand li away, speak of virtue and return to righteousness—this is the arrival of great peace.
sentences.idiomatic: Yelang and Kangju, lands ten thousand li off, speak of your virtue and pledge fealty—this is the threshold of true peace.

sentences.chinese: 然而功不加於百姓者，殆王心來加焉。
sentences.literal: Yet merit is not added to the hundred surnames—probably because the kingly mind has not yet been applied to them.
sentences.idiomatic: Yet the common people feel little gain—almost surely because the royal heart has not fully reached them.

sentences.chinese: 曾子曰：「尊其所聞，則高明矣；
sentences.literal: Master Zeng said: “Honor what you have heard, then you will be lofty and bright;
sentences.idiomatic: Zengzi said, “Hold high what you have heard and your vision clears;

sentences.chinese: 行其所知，則光大矣。
sentences.literal: practice what you know, then you will be great and bright.”
sentences.idiomatic: put into deed what you know and your influence widens.”

sentences.chinese: 高明光大，不在於它，在乎加之意而已。」
sentences.literal: Lofty brightness and great brightness are not in other things—they lie in applying intent to it, that is all.”
sentences.idiomatic: That height and breadth lie in nothing else but bending your will to them.”

sentences.chinese: 願陛下因用所聞，設誠於內而致行之，則三王何異哉！
sentences.literal: I hope Your Majesty will therefore use what you have heard, set sincerity within and extend it in practice—then how would the Three Kings differ!
sentences.idiomatic: If you take what you have heard and act on it in earnest, you will walk no differently from the Three Kings.

sentences.chinese: 陛下親耕籍田以為農先，夙寤晨興，憂勞萬民，思維往古，而務以求賢，此亦堯、舜之用心也，然而未雲獲者，士素不厲也。
sentences.literal: Your Majesty personally ploughs the sacred field to be first for farming, rises early from sleep and rises at dawn, worries and toils for the myriad people, thinks and considers antiquity, and strives to seek the worthy—this also is the intent of Yao and Shun; yet what has not yet been obtained is that scholars have not been trained in advance.
sentences.idiomatic: You plow the sacred field, rise before dawn, fret for the people, study antiquity, and hunt for worthies—this is the mind of Yao and Shun; what still fails is that talent is not schooled beforehand.

sentences.chinese: 夫不素養士而欲求賢，譬猶不琢玉而求文采也。
sentences.literal: As for not nurturing scholars in advance yet desiring to seek the worthy, it is like not carving jade yet desiring pattern and color.
sentences.idiomatic: To demand worthies without first raising scholars is to expect grain from an uncut stone.

sentences.chinese: 故養士之大者，莫大乎太學；
sentences.literal: Therefore among great matters in nurturing scholars, none is greater than the Imperial Academy;
sentences.idiomatic: Nothing nurtures talent like the Imperial Academy;

sentences.chinese: 太學者，賢士之所關也，教化之本原也。
sentences.literal: the Imperial Academy is the pass for worthies and gentlemen, and the root source of transformation through instruction.
sentences.idiomatic: it is the gate where talent gathers and the wellspring of moral education.

sentences.chinese: 今以一郡一國之眾，對亡應書者，是王道往往而絕也。
sentences.literal: Now with the multitude of one commandery and one kingdom, facing it there are none who answer the summons—is this not the kingly way often being cut off?
sentences.idiomatic: When a whole commandery cannot produce one man fit to answer the court’s call, the royal road has nearly snapped.

sentences.chinese: 臣願陛下興太學，置明師，以養天下之士，數考問以盡其材，則英俊宜可得矣。
sentences.literal: Your servant hopes Your Majesty will raise the Imperial Academy, set up enlightened teachers, thereby to nurture the gentlemen of all under Heaven, frequently examine and question them to exhaust their talents—then the handsome and outstanding ought to be obtainable.
sentences.idiomatic: I beg you to found the Imperial Academy, appoint clear-sighted masters, and there rear the empire’s talent, testing them often so their gifts show through—then true men will appear.

sentences.chinese: 今之郡守、縣令，民之師帥，所使承流而宣化也；
sentences.literal: Now the commandery governors and county magistrates are the people’s teachers and leaders, those whom you employ to receive the current and declare transformation;
sentences.idiomatic: Governors and magistrates are the people’s teachers and the pipes through which your transforming intent flows;

sentences.chinese: 故師帥不賢，則主德不宣，恩澤不流。
sentences.literal: therefore if teachers and leaders are not worthy, then the lord’s virtue is not declared and favor and bounty do not flow.
sentences.idiomatic: if those guides are unworthy, your virtue never reaches the grass roots and grace stops at the yamen gate.

sentences.chinese: 今吏既亡教訓於下，或不承用主上之法，暴虐百姓，與奸為市，貧窮孤弱，冤苦失職，甚不稱陛下之意。
sentences.literal: Now the clerks have already lost instruction and training for those below; some do not receive and employ the laws of the lord above, violently abuse the hundred surnames, traffic with treachery in the market, the poor, orphaned, and weak suffer injustice bitterly and lose their offices—very much not matching Your Majesty’s intent.
sentences.idiomatic: Too many local officials neither teach nor obey your laws; they bully the weak, haggle with criminals, and leave the humble wronged—far from what you intend.

sentences.chinese: 是以陰陽錯繆，氛氣棄塞，群生寡遂，黎民未濟，皆長吏不明，使至於此也。
sentences.literal: Therefore yin and yang are twisted awry, vapors fill and block, the throng of the living seldom attain their ends, the black-haired people are not saved—all because the senior clerks are not enlightened and cause it to reach this.
sentences.idiomatic: So yin and yang tangle, foul air blocks the sky, creatures fail, and the people drown—all because the men on top are blind.

sentences.chinese: 夫長吏多出於郎中、中郎，吏二千石子弟選郎吏，又以富訾，未必賢也。
sentences.literal: As for senior clerks, many emerge from gentlemen-of-the-palace and gentlemen-of-the-household; the sons and younger brothers of two-thousand-bushel officials are selected as gentleman-clerks, and again by wealth reckoning—they are not necessarily worthy.
sentences.idiomatic: Most magistrates come from court cadet posts or rich sons of high ministers—wealth, not worth, still picks many of them.

sentences.chinese: 且古所謂功者，以任官稱職為差，非謂積日累久也。
sentences.literal: Moreover what antiquity called merit was to take fitting office as the measure, not to mean piling up days and accumulating length of time.
sentences.idiomatic: Ancient “merit” meant filling a post well, not merely counting years.

sentences.chinese: 故小材雖累日，不離於小官；
sentences.literal: Therefore small talent, although days pile up, does not depart from small office;
sentences.idiomatic: Mediocre men may serve long yet never rise past petty rank;
